summary: The inguinal canal transmits the spermatic cord in males and the round ligament of the uterus in females.
The inguinal canal transmits the femoral artery in males and the round ligament in females.
The inguinal canal transmits the vas deferens alone in males and nothing in females.
Hesselbach's triangle is bounded by the inferior epigastric vessels, rectus abdominis, and inguinal ligament.
Hesselbach's triangle is bounded by the femoral artery, psoas major, and pectineal ligament.
Hesselbach's triangle is located in the femoral canal below the inguinal ligament.
A direct inguinal hernia passes through Hesselbach's triangle, medial to the inferior epigastric vessels.
A direct inguinal hernia passes lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels through the deep ring.
A direct inguinal hernia passes through the femoral canal below the inguinal ligament.
Below the arcuate line, all three aponeuroses pass anterior to the rectus abdominis, leaving no posterior sheath.
Below the arcuate line, all three aponeuroses pass posterior to the rectus abdominis.
The arcuate line marks the upper boundary of the rectus sheath.
An indirect inguinal hernia enters through the deep inguinal ring, lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels.
An indirect inguinal hernia enters medial to the inferior epigastric vessels.
An indirect hernia always presents below the inguinal ligament.
The external oblique aponeurosis forms the anterior wall of the inguinal canal throughout its length.
The internal oblique forms the anterior wall of the inguinal canal.
The transversalis fascia forms the anterior wall of the inguinal canal.
The superficial inguinal ring is an opening in the external oblique aponeurosis above the pubic tubercle.
The superficial inguinal ring is an opening in the transversalis fascia.
The superficial inguinal ring lies lateral to the deep inguinal ring.
